cards.text: {{c1::Operator overloading}} allows you to define custom behaviors for operators when used with user-defined types (classes and enums).
In C++, operators are implemented as functions with the name {{c1::operator}} followed by the symbol for the operator being overloaded.
To overload the + operator, you define a function named {{c1::operator+}}. For operator<<, the function is named {{c2::operator<<}}.

cards.front: Which C++ operators CANNOT be overloaded?
cards.back:
• Conditional operator (?:)
  • Sizeof operator
  • Scope resolution operator (::)
  • Member selector operator (.)
  • Pointer-to-member selector (.*)
  • typeid operator

cards.front: What are the three ways to implement operator overloading in C++?
cards.back:
• As a friend function
  • As a normal function
  • As a member function
Each approach has different use cases depending on the operator and access requirements.

cards.concept: Rules for operator overloading in C++
cards.explanation:
When overloading operators, you must follow these rules:
  • At least one operand must be a user-defined type (class or enum)
  • You cannot change the number of operands an operator takes
  • You cannot create new operator symbols
  • You cannot change operator precedence or associativity
  • Overloaded operators should behave intuitively (follow expected semantics)
examples: Cents operator+(const Cents& c1, const Cents& c2)  // Valid: binary +
// Cannot do: operator+(Cents& c1)  // Invalid: + requires 2 operands

cards.front: Can you change the precedence or associativity of operators when overloading them?
cards.back: No. Operator precedence and associativity are fixed in C++ and cannot be changed through overloading. For example, operator* will always have higher precedence than operator+, regardless of how you overload them.

cards.text: When overloading operators, they should behave {{c1::intuitively}} and follow the {{c2::expected semantics}} of the built-in operators. For example, operator+ should perform addition, not subtraction.

cards.front: Why can't you overload operator+ to add two ints together in a new way?
cards.back: At least one operand of an overloaded operator must be a user-defined type (class or enum). Since int is a fundamental type, you cannot redefine how operators work for built-in types only. This prevents modification of fundamental language behavior.

cards.front: Do overloaded operators need to return a specific type in C++?
cards.back: No, overloaded operators can return any type you want (or void). However, for intuitive behavior, they should typically return types consistent with built-in operators. For example, operator+ usually returns the result type, while operator<< returns a stream reference.

cards.question: What does this operator overload do?
cards.answer: Overloads the + operator as a member function to add two Cents objects together, returning a new Cents object with the sum of their m_cents values.
cards.explanation: This is a member function implementation. The left operand is the implicit *this object, and the right operand is the rhs parameter.

Operator overloading makes code more intuitive and readable:
  • Natural mathematical notation: a + b instead of a.add(b)
  • Consistency with built-in types
  • More concise code
  • Enables use of generic algorithms and templates
However, operators should only be overloaded when they provide clear, intuitive meaning.
examples: Cents total = c1 + c2;  // Clear: adding money
// Bad: Don't use + for unrelated operations
